insight: In sets with maximum absorb and maximum soak (e.g. with Infusion Grid), Apollo is highly prioritized because it provides permanent value that does not deplete drone supply and bypasses absorb/barrier defenses. Conventional attackers are poor investments in such sets because they get sniped or ram into big absorb inefficiently.
author: consensus
context: Discussion about optimal opener strategy in high-absorb/high-soak sets featuring Apollo, with spiritfryer, lyra1712, and xyotsuba all contributing.

insight: In sets with Apollo plus maximum absorb and soak, the recommended opener priority hierarchy is: drones first, then minimize gold float, then rush Apollo, with everything else subordinate. This approach tends to force 4+ Engineers due to awkward gold amounts remaining after drone and tech purchases.
author: spiritfryer
context: spiritfryer theorizing about opener priorities in sets with Apollo, max absorb, and max soak, supported loosely by xyotsuba's follow-up.

insight: A bull-all-in strategy versus Lycomedes was found through analysis to convincingly outperform a more conservative line (Ampo), confirming the bull line as a strong hedge against natural Animus development. The Ampo buy was identified as the mistake in that game.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers analyzed a game between community members and reported back that the bull all-in line vs Lycomedes was clearly correct over the Ampo line.

insight: Gaussite Pivot (also called 'Gaussite Pivot' or 'Gauss Pivot') is an experimental unit that Player 1 starts the game with at no cost, designed as a balance mechanism to reduce the inherent disadvantage P1 faces in green-heavy sets due to natural Conduit timing favoring P2. It is currently only available in 'Mode of the Week' events, not the base game.
author: silentslayers
context: A user asked why Gaussite Pivot is not in the base game; silentslayers explained its purpose and current availability.

insight: The community identifies that P1 faces a structural disadvantage in green-heavy sets due to P2's natural Conduit timing, and that this is one of the recognized asymmetries that proposals like Gaussite Pivot aim to address. Adding Gaussite Pivot to the base game is considered a live option pending lead developer (Elyot) involvement.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion about potential base game balance changes and the role of Gaussite Pivot as a P1 green-set equalizer.

insight: Expert player silentslayers reports being able to identify the favored player in approximately 50% or more of Base Set + 8 sets by inspection, but finds it significantly harder to determine in Base Set + 10 or higher due to the increased set complexity and number of viable lines.
author: silentslayers
context: Discussion on whether players can reasonably assess set balance for the purpose of a bidding/handicap system.

insight: A specific game with 'P1 Odin' (Omega Splitter in P1 hands) was assessed by silentslayers as an unwinnable set for P2: even building an Omega Splitter early and triple-walling does not save P2, and silentslayers conceded before a single attacker was purchased, illustrating extreme first-player advantage in that configuration.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers shared a game they conceded very early as P2 in a set featuring Omega Splitter heavily favoring P1, and responded to pikachumemes asking whether rushing Antima Comet before the Omega Splitter timing could work.

insight: When evaluating handicap bids (extra gold on a given turn), the granularity of turns matters: the ordering of handicap strength is approximately p1+gold on turn 1 > ... > p1+gold on turn N > no handicap > p2+gold on turn N > ... > p2+gold on turn 1. A handicap given to the winning player is not meaningful, so only the disadvantaged side needs to be compensated.
author: lyra1712
context: Theoretical discussion about a gold-handicap bidding system for set fairness, with lyra1712 laying out the handicap ordering and spiritfryer stress-testing edge cases.

insight: Community expert pikachumemes notes that a 1-gold handicap given on turns 2 or 3 in Base Set Only (BSO) would simply enable the 'Elyot' opening (an aggressive early-game line), which may be degenerate and not healthier than current BSO gameplay, suggesting that gold handicaps could inadvertently enable non-standard broken lines rather than just balancing normal play.
author: pikachumemes.
context: Discussion about applying gold-handicap bidding systems to BSO, with pikachumemes warning that bids could unlock degenerate openings.
